posts.caption:
Since launching in 2022, @skyhighfarmgoods — co-founded by Gagosian-represented artist Dan Colen and ex- @commedesgarcons & @doverstreetmarketnewyork alum Daphne Seybold — has flexed its fashion bona fides, cycling through partnerships like crop rotations; it’s released everything from @balenciaga workwear  to @supremenewyork beanies.
For its latest partnership with @levis , SHFG created an eight-piece denim collection with an appropriately on-brand concept. “Our goal was to create the perfect uniform for modern farmers: pieces that are functional, comfortable, and built for real work, while still feeling contemporary and relevant.”
SHFG’s whimsical take on workwear offers a fresh POV on Levi’s classics, but it’s the message behind the capsule that’s key: “With this collection, you can love your jeans knowing they support farmers as they transition to certified organic cotton. We also want you to get out there and get your hands (and jeans) dirty..”, says Matty Friedman
How did it take us until now to get Levi’s for farmers? Well, maybe it just took the farming mindset to create the perfect farmer’s closet, but with a Sky High twist via tough touches.
Read the full interview with SHFG at the link in bio.
Written by: @chriserikthomas
Images: Levi’s X SHFG
posts.alt_text:
Introducing our final cover star of the spring issue of HIGHSNOBIETY, @kaytranada. Kay is the type of artist that just gets you off your feet, whose beats take you through the movement. His success has amounted to Grammys, collaborations with artists such as @kaliuchis, and a change to the whole electronic scene. 

What comes as a surprise is that it was all a “happy accident,” as the artist puts it. Kay’s real passion lay in the creation of dark and moody sample-driven hip-hop. Montreal shit, as he so succinctly put it — and the very music he grew up with.  We spoke to Kaytranda about his journey, and how he’s ready to go back to his roots and burrow into the underground with plans to drop “Mixtapes, beat tapes, EPs.” Kaytranada will be here: tinkering, listening, conjuring something unlike anything that came before.   

Read more about Kay and how he’s doing things his own way at the link in bio. 

By @pxthompson
Photographed by @nad_1n3
Styled by @thevonford
Photo Assistant @franziskusd
Hair & Makeup @adiamhabtezion
Video: @andrea___mena, @byeemj, @y.lanyi 
Casting & Talent Manager: @dasia.schow 
Styling Assistant: @the.daily.zazz
Seamstress @_a.r.a.i_
Production Asst: @fuckhughman

posts.caption:
Pharrell loves the way it looks when water beads off waterproof clothing. ⁠
⁠
We know this because for Fall/Winter 2026, he created jackets that appear permanently wet, beads of fake rain eternally trickling down their body, as well as inventing a new weatherproof @louisvuitton fabric that’ll genuinely repel any rain it faces. ⁠
⁠
The innovation is called “Silk Tech.” And yes, it’s made of actual silk, in varying degrees. The full debut Silk Tech range is up for pre-order on Louis Vuitton’s website, alongside more FW26 goods including a 100% silk “Technical Shell Jacket” that’s completely waterproof, has taped seams and rubberized zippers yet confusingly isn’t part of the “silk tech” collection.⁠
⁠
Swipe for a closer look.

posts.caption:
Paris has a new coffee destination, and it's dressed in @ysl. ⁠
⁠
The fashion house has officially opened a café in its new Avenue Montaigne boutique. The café will serve everything from coffee, matcha, and pastries, but it's the interior that speaks to Saint Laurent's signature vision. ⁠
⁠
Also featured are sleek interiors, vibrant furniture, a terrace, and even a library with a curated selection of reads. Swipe for a closer look.⁠
⁠
📷️: @twoarabminds
posts.alt_text:
Why do @newbalancerunning sneakers and @usmmodularfurniture ‘s modular furniture make so much sense together? Because good design complements good design.

Quietly debuted at New Balance’s seasonal Paris showroom in June, a bespoke USM furniture collaboration — revealed exclusively by Highsnobiety — elegantly displayed New Balance’s newest designs, like a collector showcasing their personal stash of art. Or sneakers, for that matter. New Balance’s residency at the Hôtel de Bourrienne. New Balance’s residency at the Hôtel de Bourrienne extended beyond the collaboration with a collection of homewares to complement the partnership including gaming sets to an endless assortment of cups. 

“The goal of this was to find a way to engineer an experience true to both brands,” consultant Scott Watts says. “A premium modular system for premium products.”

Written by: trasparenti
Images: New Balance X USM

posts.caption:
When is a sandal a sandal? And when is it simply a bit of rope tying your toes down to a slim little sole like a Greek-passing extra on the set of The Odyssey? This is the question we need to ask ourselves now, because there’s a max-minimal sandal on the rise that somehow makes the Havaianas flip-flop look conservative (and Birkenstock downright matronly).⁠
⁠
Call it the Slutty Little Sandal, if you will. It melds a simple system of cord straps with a petite footbed for a streamlined silhouette that meets the moment — specifically, the meltingly hot moment, when all we want to do is wear as little as possible, especially on our trotters. ⁠
⁠
It’s been the $49.99 Xero Shoes Genesis that’s driving the rise of this barely-there style, made by a company whose origin story begins not with a runway but rather on the reality TV series Shark Tank. Hit the link in bio to read more.

For its latest partnership with @levis , SHFG created an eight-piece denim collection with an appropriately on-brand concept. “Our goal was to create the perfect uniform for modern farmers: pieces that are functional, comfortable, and built for real work, while still feeling contemporary and relevant.”

SHFG’s whimsical take on workwear offers a fresh POV on Levi’s classics, but it’s the message behind the capsule that’s key: “With this collection, you can love your jeans knowing they support farmers as they transition to certified organic cotton. We also want you to get out there and get your hands (and jeans) dirty..”, says Matty Friedman

How did it take us until now to get Levi’s for farmers? Well, maybe it just took the farming mindset to create the perfect farmer’s closet, but with a Sky High twist via tough touches. 

Read the full interview with SHFG at the link in bio.

Written by: @chriserikthomas 
Images: Levi’s X SHFG

posts.caption:
@Lacoste’s Crocoliga table tennis tournament is officially underway. The opening event took place in Berlin, hosted by creative studio @obby.jappari. Huge congratulations to the winner @kilian__held who will be heading to the finals in September.⁠
⁠
With tennis all the talk right now, Crocoliga, a partnership initiative by Lacoste, @Zalando, and Highsnobiety, offers a different take on racket sports. One that's less about tradition and rankings, and more about community, creativity, and the joy of play.⁠
⁠
Who'll be in Paris for the next tryout?
posts.alt_text:
Since launching in 2022, @skyhighfarmgoods — co-founded by Gagosian-represented artist Dan Colen and ex- @commedesgarcons & @doverstreetmarketnewyork alum Daphne Seybold — has flexed its fashion bona fides, cycling through partnerships like crop rotations; it’s released everything from @balenciaga workwear  to @supremenewyork beanies. 

For its latest partnership with @levis , SHFG created an eight-piece denim collection with an appropriately on-brand concept. “Our goal was to create the perfect uniform for modern farmers: pieces that are functional, comfortable, and built for real work, while still feeling contemporary and relevant.”

SHFG’s whimsical take on workwear offers a fresh POV on Levi’s classics, but it’s the message behind the capsule that’s key: “With this collection, you can love your jeans knowing they support farmers as they transition to certified organic cotton. We also want you to get out there and get your hands (and jeans) dirty..”, says Matty Friedman

How did it take us until now to get Levi’s for farmers? Well, maybe it just took the farming mindset to create the perfect farmer’s closet, but with a Sky High twist via tough touches.

posts.caption:
From styling her own red carpet looks to journaling through music, @odessaazion has always done things her own way.
Since her breakout role in ‘Marty Supreme,’ the actor has stepped foot on all the major red carpets, been at the center of wig rumors (just like every celeb with great hair) and begun carving out a name for herself in fashion.
Now, alongside season two of ‘I Love LA’ and the upcoming psychological thriller ‘Fonda,’ A’zion is entering a new era as the face of @crocs latest campaign.
We caught up with the multi-hyphenate to talk style, vintage grails, making music and why being “wonderfully unordinary” beats fitting in every time. Head to the link in bio to read the full interview.

posts.caption:
Summer doesn’t arrive all at once. It unfolds in moods, moments, and memories. ☀️🌿
@ahsaassy_ steps into the season’s softest chapter, where easy silhouettes and unhurried afternoons become a reminder that slowing down is its own kind of luxury.
In collaboration with @aurelia_womenswear , this explores the evolving moods of summer, from nature’s quiet embrace to the intensity of the afternoon sun, and the effortless cool of golden evenings.✨
This is a celebration of the season in all its shades, beginning with the calm before the heat, where style isn’t about standing out, but feeling completely at home in your own skin.

posts.caption:
@jennifer.garner *always* knows what to do (and it usually involves sorghum).
Between starring in summer’s buzziest new series, taking her near-billion dollar company public, and turning ‘Pretend Cooking Show’ into a reality, she still manages to be the nicest woman in Hollywood.
Playing a widowed food blogger in @Peacock’s adaptation of @elinhilderbrand’s bestseller ‘The Five-Star Weekend,’ the beloved actress and entrepreneur channels her inner Ina Garten for a not-so-Instagrammable girls’ weekend on Nantucket with co-stars-turned-IRL besties, @chloesevigny, @morereginahall, @gemmachan and @darcycarden, exploring what it means to grow and develop in middle age.
At 54, the Marvel Hero and supermom reflects on her genre-spanning career, being “total besties” with John Cena, her surprisingly vast knowledge of weaponry, plus the responsibility of celebrities to stand up for working locally for InStyle’s Summer Imagemakers Issue at the link in @instylemagazine’s bio now.

posts.caption:
Go crazy, and show a little calf this summer!
Whether you love them or you hate them, let's all welcome back the trend that defined the Y2K years: Capris. They first made a splash in the ’50s and ’60s (think Audrey Hepburn and Marilyn Monroe), enjoyed a resurgence in the ’90s and ’00s (think Hilary Duff and Rihanna), then retreated into the shadows in the 2010s. But thanks to stars like #Beyoncé, #BellaHadid, and #HaileyBieber, the pant is back in full force. Swipe through to see some of our fave capri looks from the past and present, and go to the link in bio to shop some of InStyle's picks for the best pairs for the warmer months.
📷: @gettyimages, Courtesy of Saint Laurent

posts.caption:
To celebrate the global soccer tournament kicking off, the American Crew Event at Hypebeast Cup explored legendary grooming moments. Utilizing American Crew products, @curtistheebarber provided exclusive styling sessions, while Richard Mendoza aka @richthebarber delivered game-inspired cuts to clients. @percival_menswear designer @christophergove was also on site for a panel talk about modern grooming and styling trends. The entire experience showcased how soccer and fashion naturally intersect, especially with the exclusive American Crew soccer jerseys making their appearance.
In partnership with @americancrew

posts.caption:
We’re giving away a premium, Japan-inspired Toki jersey that embodies how true cultural connection transcends translation. This exclusive piece beautifully translates soccer’s global language into an expression of fine Japanese craft. The striking white long-sleeve kit showcases crisp red piping along the raglan seams and an artful, line-drawn panorama capturing Tokyo’s iconic architectural cityscape along the base. Follow the instructions below for the chance to take one home.
1. Like this post
2. Follow our page and the official @suntorytoki account
3. Tag a friend
Open to US residents only. Terms and conditions apply (link in bio).
In partnership with @suntorytoki

posts.caption:
Five years after their initial “Absorbed Language” release, @salehebembury and StudioPROBA @alexproba reunite for a new chapter titled “Soft Imprints”.
This 2026 limited-edition collection translates Bembury’s signature topographical fingerprint motifs into Alex Proba’s world of abstract, organic shapes and bold colors. The result is a highly tactile dialogue between product design and spatial art, turning everyday items into expressive centerpieces.
Acting as both functional decor and collectible objects, the drop is strictly limited to just 200 pillows and 80 rugs. The collection will be available via both artists’ platforms. 🛋️🎨
Photo: @alexproba

posts.caption:
His work has brought him to countless destinations across the globe, but @TravisScott still has one major stop left on his bucket list. “I hit every continent on the last tour except Antarctica,” he says. “It’s kind of crazy.”
For Scott, travel is about more than seeing new places—it’s about expanding perspective, and he encourages leaning into exploration whenever possible. “Go touch earth and go actually interact with this world,” he says. “It could change your mindset.”
Read more about Scott’s adventures at the link in our bio.

posts.alt_text:
Introducing our global cover star, @TravisScott!

The Houston-born artist has spent the past year moving between worlds, crafting the follow-up to 2023’s ‘Utopia’ from his Malibu studio while preparing for his much-anticipated acting debut as a bard in Christopher Nolan’s @theodysseymovie, in theaters July 17.

From music to film to fashion, Scott’s creative universe continues to expand. He also stars alongside Owen Wilson in the upcoming comedy ‘Rolling Loud’ and maintains long-running partnerships with @Nike and @Oakley, all while leading @cactusjack into its next chapter.

Yet at the center of it all remains the music. “I’ve been mad inspired over the last year and a half,” Scott says. “I’ve been buckled down and back building beats from scratch, just creating and pushing the sound.”

Read our full cover story at the link in bio as Scott opens up about the creative forces that drive him, his big-screen debut, and the importance of self-care.

Global Chief Content Officer @giampietrobaudo

posts.caption:
The 2026 July/August issue is here!
At the center of this year’s Summer Entertainment Issue is @TravisScott, who opens up about working on the follow-up to 2023’s ‘Utopia,’ the travels and experiences fueling his next chapter, and making his acting debut as part of a star-studded ensemble in Christopher Nolan’s ‘The Odyssey.’
As much as we love a blockbuster with a stacked A-list cast, championing fresh talent is always top of mind. Inside the issue, we spotlight eight rising stars whose work this summer demands your attention, from ‘Outer Banks’ star @MadisonBaileyBabe to Avantika of ‘Not Suitable for Work’ to @sashacalle of ‘Sugar.’
It’s also a blockbuster time for literary escapes, as the rise of #BookTok turns reading into an unexpected status symbol that’s shaping summer travel. Looking ahead to the Fall collections, we explore the age-old question of whether sex still sells, given the state of undress on the runway, red carpet, and social feeds in 2026. Stay tuned for the issue hitting newsstands this month.

posts.caption:
While we may be L’OFFICIEL USA, we have deep ties with our French friends across the Atlantic. #BastilleDay marks the beginning of the French Revolution and celebrates the resilience, spirit, and culture of France. For us, it’s also the perfect excuse to embrace timeless red, white, and blue—with unmistakable French elegance.
From a red-and-white striped dress on our 1956 cover to a baby blue gingham look from a 1986 editorial, we revisited the L’OFFICIEL archives to uncover summer style that still feels effortlessly chic in 2025. Just ten days after the Fourth of July, there’s no need to retire your nautical stripes, vibrant reds, or classic blues—they’re a summer staple for a reason.
After all, the French did invent chic. Swipe through some of our favorite Bastille Day-inspired looks from the L’OFFICIEL archives in celebration of tomorrow’s fête. Read more at the link in bio.
Photos courtesy of L’OFFICIEL Archives
#bastilledayfashion #frenchstyle #summerfashion

posts.caption:
When @BellaMaclean first stepped onto the ‘Rivals’ set, she knew she was part of something special—but it wasn’t until the first table read that it truly sank in that she would be bringing the world of Jilly Cooper’s iconic ‘Rutshire Chronicles’ to life.  “I remember being like, ‘Oh my God. We’re really making this, and this is actually really happening, and this isn’t a dream,’” she tells L’OFFICIEL.
As Taggie, Maclean inhabits a young woman finding her voice, ambition, and independence. “So much of the time, you want to portray gritty, complicated characters. They’re really fun to play, but I like that Taggie is morally strong,” she says. “She strives for goodness, and it really makes you reflect on how you act in your own life,”
The character certainly left an impact on the actor, who will reprise her role when the second half of Season 2 drops in November. “As the series goes on, she becomes a lot more ballsy, and I felt myself becoming more strong-willed too,” says Maclean. “I took a little piece of her with me.” Read more of our conversation with Bella Maclean at the link in bio.
Editor-in-Chief of @lofficielusa @caroline_grosso⁠
Photography @emilysoto
Styled by @calvy.click

Hair @chadmaxwellhair @rachelpolycarpe

posts.alt_text:
Un rovescio a una mano, raro nel tennis di oggi, e un gioco elegante e variato: @Lore_Musetti, 24 anni, ha aperto il 2026 conquistando il quinto posto del ranking ATP, prima che un infortunio alla gamba sinistra lo costringesse a rinunciare al Roland Garros. Global ambassador di #BottegaVeneta, il tennista si racconta a @LOfficielItalia, ritratto dagli scatti di @RaffGrosso e con lo styling di @Fernando.Echeverria_, vestendo un total look della collezione #Fall2026, firmato dalla direttrice creativa @Louise_Trotter_. Tra scaramanzie da spogliatoio, il bronzo olimpico di Parigi, l'ammirazione per il tennis di Alcaraz e la passione per la cucina: «Colpire la pallina, il suono che fa quando la colpisco: è questo che mi rende felice». Leggi l’intervista a cura di @Fabia.DiDrusco al link in bio e sul nuovo numero de @LOfficielItalia, ora in edicola.

@lore_musetti

Tra scaramanzie da spogliatoio, il bronzo olimpico di Parigi, l'ammirazione per il tennis di Alcaraz e la passione per la cucina: «Colpire la pallina, il suono che fa quando la colpisco: è questo che mi rende felice». Leggi l’intervista a cura di @Fabia.DiDrusco al link in bio e sul nuovo numero de @LOfficielItalia, ora in edicola.

posts.caption:
@Balenciaga dropped its Spring Summer 2026 preview collection in Mumbai last night, and the energy in the room felt different.
With Fatima Sana Sheikh, Bobby Deol, Mouni Roy, Sanya Malhotra, Malavika Mohanan, and Krithi Shetty in attendance, it felt less like a formal showcase and more like a real moment around fashion. The room was sharp, observant, and engaged.
Fatima Sana Shaikh said it best: “Balenciaga always has a chic approach and the brand is now heading towards it even more aggressively.” And that tone carried through the evening. Nothing felt loud for attention, yet everything commanded it.
What stood out was how people responded. There was curiosity, closer inspection, and genuine interest in the details. Some silhouettes felt familiar but more refined, while others brought a quiet sense of drama without trying too hard.
That is when a collection works.
Not when it demands attention, but when it earns it.
@balenciaga #Balenciaga

posts.caption:
Hermès has once again turned its equestrian heritage into wearable art with Into The Horsescape, the House’s latest high jewellery collection.
Designed by Creative Director Pierre Hardy, the collection reimagines iconic equestrian elements—from horse bits and harnesses to the fluid movement of a galloping horse—into extraordinary high jewellery. Sculptural necklaces, cuffs, rings, and body pieces transform centuries of Hermès heritage into bold contemporary creations, proving that true luxury is about evolving tradition rather than simply preserving it.
More than a jewellery collection, Into The Horsescape is a masterclass in craftsmanship, creativity, and storytelling, where the language of Hermès is expressed through precious stones, exceptional design, and timeless artistry.
@hermes #Hermes #Luxurious

posts.caption:
Apollo has finally unveiled the first production example of its outrageous Evo hypercar—and it’s unlike anything else on the road.
Named Caribbean Dragon, the one-off customer commission is finished in a bespoke Pearl White and Ocean Blue paint scheme inspired by tropical waters, a process that took more than 1,000 hours to complete. The track-focused hypercar features over 75 hand-finished carbon-fibre body panels, a dramatic fighter jet-inspired design, and a 3D-printed titanium “Dragon Skin” exhaust capable of withstanding temperatures of up to 1,000°C.
Power comes from a naturally aspirated 6.3-litre V12 producing 800PS, paired with a six-speed sequential gearbox, allowing the 1,300kg hypercar to sprint from 0–100 km/h in just 2.7 seconds before reaching a top speed of 335 pkm/h. With only 10 Apollo Evos being built worldwide, the Caribbean Dragon represents one of the most exclusive and visually dramatic hypercars ever created.

posts.caption:
Dior has transformed the heart of Taormina into a Mediterranean escape with its latest Dioriviera Resort pop-up.
Overlooking the Sicilian coastline, the immersive boutique blends fashion, art, and travel through vibrant summer installations, handcrafted décor, and the House’s signature Toile de Jouy motifs. Designed as a celebration of the Dioriviera collection, the space reflects Christian Dior’s vision of couture as a “refuge of the marvelous,” where luxury extends far beyond fashion into unforgettable experiences.
More than a seasonal boutique, the Taormina pop-up showcases how Dior continues to redefine luxury retail by turning destinations into immersive worlds of craftsmanship, culture, and escape.

posts.caption:
#ManifestCover
Marriage didn’t change Sonakshi Sinha (@aslisona) and Zaheer Iqbal (@iamzahero). It simply gave their love a new address.
“We dated for seven years and knew everything about each other before marriage,” says Zaheer.
“That’s why we got married,” Sonakshi adds. “We wanted to learn to co-exist, to live in harmony and happiness.”
Perhaps that’s what makes their story feel so effortless. Not the absence of disagreements, but the certainty that they’ll always find their way back to each other.
Our July-August issue is now live on Magzter. Read the full cover story at the link in bio.

posts.alt_text:
#ManifestCover

There’s an ease between Sonakshi Sinha (@aslisona) and Zaheer Iqbal (@iamzahero) that the camera catches instantly.

From a fitted evening gown set off by black onyx earrings with emerald and polki diamonds to a striking deep-red jacket paired with a tourmaline choker centred on a nine-carat rose-cut diamond—both from P.C. Totuka & Sons—every frame is a study in effortless elegance.
Because for Sonakshi, adornment is instinct. “Diamonds over pearls for me, definitely,” she asserts. “I like minimalistic pieces. But I also love vintage ones.”

posts.caption:
#ManifestCover
Two years into marriage, nine into love. For Sonakshi Sinha (@aslisona) and Zaheer Iqbal (@iamzahero), togetherness has never been about grand gestures. It’s love distilled to its essentials—a shared home, an easy silence, and the same laughter that started it all.
For Manifest’s July-August cover, the couple lets us in on what “forever” actually looks like, unguarded and unhurried. “I think people really tend to scare you about how your life is going to change after marriage,” says Sonakshi, adding, “but the beauty of it, for us at least, is that things haven’t changed at all. It still feels like I’m his girlfriend and he’s my boyfriend.”
Between banter and blessing, stillness and spontaneity, they’ve found the rare thing most couples spend a lifetime chasing. A forever kind of love.
Our latest issue is now live on Magzter. Read the full cover story at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
This was never about finding the grandest destination. It was about finding the place that felt most soulful.
With their closest loved ones witnessing the moment, TV actor Palak Purswani (@palak.purswani) and Rohan Khanna (@rohankhanna1708), Vice President & National Head–Product, Pricing & Marketing at Reliance Jio, took their seven sacred vows at sunrise in front of the iconic 112-foot Adiyogi statue at Sadhguru’s Isha Yoga Center in Coimbatore. The ceremony was held with special permission from the Isha Foundation, making it an exceptionally rare and deeply meaningful occasion.
It was reportedly the first wedding to be held on the premises.
Their nuptials made for a serene wedding to remember, while also bringing into fresh spotlight the beautiful tradition of temple weddings in India.
Read the full story through the link in our bio.
By Ismat Tahseen (@ismat.tahseen)
📷: @knottingbells
#Manifest #Wedding

posts.caption:
The Hyundai India Couture Week x FDCI 2026 (@fdciofficial) calendar is officially here, and this season is already changing the narrative.
Taking place from July 23–30, this year’s edition opens in Hyderabad for the very first time, with Anamika Khanna presenting at the iconic Falaknuma Palace before the showcase moves to Delhi.
Expect heirloom craftsmanship, maximalist embellishment, razor-sharp tailoring, theatrical silhouettes and the kind of fashion moments that remind us why Indian couture continues to command global attention. If fashion month had a desi counterpart, this would be it.
See you front row... or refreshing the feed every five minutes.
Read the full story via the link in our bio🔗
By Akshay Kaushal

posts.caption:
#ManifestCover
Their anniversary trip may have started with a plan to visit Tanzania, but they ended up going to a different destination—Phuket . That’s exactly how Sonakshi Sinha (@aslisona) and Zaheer Iqbal (@iamzahero) like to travel—with curiosity leading the way.
“We hadn’t been there together, so we said, let’s go,” Sonakshi recalls.
They’re also just getting started.
“We made a pact to explore places we’ve never been,” they say. “New cities, new countries, new continents.”
For them, the best souvenirs aren’t shopping bags. They are shared memories waiting to be made.
Our July-August issue is now live on Magzter. Read the full cover story at the link in bio.

posts.alt_text:
#ManifestCover

Marriage didn’t change Sonakshi Sinha (@aslisona) and Zaheer Iqbal (@iamzahero). It simply gave their love a new address.

“We dated for seven years and knew everything about each other before marriage,” says Zaheer.

“That’s why we got married,” Sonakshi adds. “We wanted to learn to co-exist, to live in harmony and happiness.”
Perhaps that’s what makes their story feel so effortless. Not the absence of disagreements, but the certainty that they’ll always find their way back to each other.

Our July-August issue is now live on Magzter. Read the full cover story at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
A reader wrote to our fashion critic: "I have my mother’s satin wedding gown, worn on Oct. 25, 1941 — just six weeks before Pearl Harbor. It’s magnificent, with long sleeves, ruching, satin buttons and a long skirt. I wore it in 1979 and had it preserved, hopefully for a future generation. That has not come to pass, and I struggle with what to do. I can’t see giving it to Salvation Army. Apparently it cannot be dyed. I’ve talked to a tailor about using the skirt material to make one or two blouses. It feels as if it should go into some kind of costume museum, but it’s not a big name designer. Am I being too sentimental?"
Clothes are powerful wormholes to our memories and emotions, which is why it can be so hard to throw them away. But is there liberation in letting go of the past? At the link in our bio, read @vvfriedman's advice. Photo via Jainel Morris
posts.alt_text:
The summer edition of haute couture isn’t for the faint of heart. It’s always hot outside, and heading indoors seldom provides much relief. Spare a thought for the models, too: Couture looks are often bigger and more complicated than ready-to-wear clothes (the collections shown in July are for the fall season, so bigger, more complicated and heavier).

But in Dior’s actual runway show, the collection felt anything but stifling. There was a similar sensibility in the Schiaparelli collection earlier in the day, but something about the air flow at the Musée Rodin put the lightness front and center. Chanel seemed equally concerned with comfort, with several looks featuring light, sheer tops. At Jean Paul Gaultier, some of the biggest couture-type creations ever sent down a runway were augmented with bare shoulders and bare backs.

See more Paris Couture Week looks from the runway and onlookers at the link in our bio. Visuals by @simbarashecha

posts.caption:
Pierpaolo Piccioli’s debut couture show for Balenciaga was a gorgeous parade of dramatic designs. The collection was recognized by many as a return to form, and while it was "beautiful, no question," as our chief fashion critic Vanessa Friedman wrote in her review, it also felt familiar.
Piccioli has embraced the extraordinary color sense that became his signature in his former job at Valentino, as well as the grace of form. He has combined that with classic Balenciaga shapes (the sack, the trapeze, the balloon skirt) made in an entirely contemporary way. On the surface, the designer is doing everything right. But is it right for right now?
Read @vvfriedman's full review of @balenciaga's Paris couture show at the link in our bio. Photos by @simbarashecha

posts.caption:
Duran Lantink, the next-gen designer at Jean Paul Gaultier and the first permanent creative director to follow the brand’s founder, is a master provocateur.
It was Lantink, after all, who broke the internet last spring when he opened and closed a show for his own line with a man wearing a female chest prosthetic and a woman wearing a man’s. In October, he featured a bodysuit silk-screened with a naked male body, detailed genitalia and all, in his first Gaultier ready-to-wear show. Little wonder his Gaultier couture was the most-anticipated debut this week.
Nowadays, as fashion shows become more about driving engagement and name recognition, the more extreme and ridiculous a look, the better. And Lantink isn’t the only designer who seemingly strives for virality. Armani Privé, Robert Wun, Viktor and Rolf and Schiaparelli all made their bids for the attention economy. But some gimmicks work better than others. Tap the link in our bio to read @vvfriedman's analysis of the couture shows in Paris. Photos by @simbarashecha, Jean Paul Gaultier, Luca Tombolini, Umberto Fratini

posts.caption:
Over three weeks and across four different cities, our photographer @simbarashecha went to World Cup matches featuring teams from five continents, capturing the best soccer style.
Though the sport has yet to move fully into the American mainstream, the tournament’s return to U.S. soil has brought along with it breathtaking levels of enthusiasm in team identity. With flags, fringe, face paint and at least one horned helmet, fans from every country brought unique style and flair to the party.
Swipe through to see the World Cup fans who left it all on the pitch, and tap the link in our bio to see more photos.

posts.caption:
A reader asked our fashion critic: "I understand that we’re all dressing more casually these days, especially so since the pandemic. But there is something special about going to a play or a nice restaurant, and increasingly I’m seeing folks at such places dressed in what they’d wear to the supermarket or to do chores around the house — which kind of makes me sad. Are we losing something?"
The rising casualness of the world is making us reconsider what getting “dressed up” means. Is that a bad thing? At the link in our bio, read @vvfriedman's answer. Photo by Jeremy Moeller/Getty Images

posts.caption:
India's Renee Noronha has won first place in the 18-24 category at the Ironman Philippines. Completing the gruelling challenge at 15:55:37, the 20-year-old IIT Madras student upheld held her record of being the youngest Indian female Ironman finisher.
Renee battled torrential rains and unforgiving temperatures to complete the race. In conversation with SheThePeople, the triathlete also revealed that she participated on day 3 of her period, making the victory a profound reminder of her strength and resilience as a woman.
With this win, Renee has now qualified for the Ironman World Championship Hawaii, scheduled for October 2026. She is also on a mission to set a new Guinness world record to become the youngest person in the world to do a full Ironman on 6 continents. She is on track to achieve this by April 2027, at the age of 21.
Read full interview at www.shethepeople.tv. | @renee.noronha
#Ironman #Philippines #sports full Ironman challenge, women in sports, women in endurance sports, mumbai sports, Chennai sports, IIT Madras student, women in STEM, ironman Kona, triathlon, marathon, menstrual awareness
posts.alt_text:
FIFA has officially selected and accredited Assamese photojournalist Gitika Talukdar to cover the upcoming 2026 FIFA World Cup. This will be her third time covering the Men's FIFA World Cup.

Prior to this, Gitika has covered the 2018 Russia World Cup, the 2022 Qatar FIFA World Cup, the 2019 France Women's FIFA World Cup, the 2023 Women's FIFA World Cup in Australia and New Zealand, the 2020 Tokyo Olympic Games, the Paris Olympic Games, and others.

Visit www.shethepeople.tv for more

posts.caption:
India’s batter Yastika Bhatiya added a new milestone in women’s cricket as she became the first-ever woman to score a century at Lord’s. Yastika produced a sublime maiden Test hundred, scoring 113  off 158 balls.
The cricketer credits Smriti Mandhana’s support after her comeback. “I had a conversation with her, and she just looked at me and said that this is going to be a turning point in your career,” Bhatia said.
Visit www.shethepeople.tv to read more
#cricket women's cricket, India, Yastika Bhatiya, Smriti Mandhana, trending, viral, test match

posts.caption:
Yui Kamiji defeats Diede de Groot in straight sets to win the Grand Slam.
This victory was particularly significant as it fulfilled her stated goal for the 2026 season. Winning the Wimbledon singles title was the one major missing from her trophy cabinet, completing her career "Golden Slam".

posts.caption:
A college girl from Kolkata decided to take her first steps in the entertainment industry, albeit being besieged by loads of ‘what ifs’. What if she doesn’t get projects? What if her introverted behaviour prevents her from acting? Putting all these questions aside, she nursed her passion for being an actor.
That’s Tillotama Shome, an actor recognised for winning the trust of people, a storyteller who doesn’t hesitate in refusing scripts she doesn’t align with.
Born to parents who taught her the understanding of affection and freedom, she always aimed to be financially capable. She began her career with the film ‘Monsoon Wedding’ and built a career defined by versatility rather than convention.
Her feature film Boksho Bondi, also produced by her, was recently awarded by FIPRESCI (The International Federation of Film Critics).
Visit www.shethepeople.tv to read the full interview by @_shivanilamba
#tillotamashome #bollywood #cinema Boksho Bondi, actress, body objectification, storytelling, film characters, entertainment industry, trending viral, Monsoon Wedding, Qissa, Acting

posts.caption:
The perfect way to unwind after a long day? Light a candle. Make some tea. Curl up with a good book, let the words wash over you, drift into something quiet and nourishing.
No. Open Instagram. Scroll until the candle burns out. Somewhere along the way, this became the unwind.
The most convenient explanation for this is that we’ve all rotted. The Instagram algorithm has hijacked our dopamine. Our attention spans have shrunk to the length of a YouTube Short. And us? Passive recipients of whatever the feed decides we deserve.
Visit www.shethepeople.tv to read more.
#brainrot Social media, brainrot content, Ananya Pandey, doom scroll, Instagram, trending, viral, explore, books, reading, trends, attention span

posts.caption:
Justin Bieber for Issue 01: “The First Ever” Outlander Magazine In-Print!
This is a real piece of history for us all. The past 10 years of lessons and learning have led up to this moment and I couldn’t be happier to share it with you all.
Some of you may be new followers who discovered us last week and some of you may have been here since day 1, either way this is for everyone who aligns with what it means to be an outlander.
Also, this is a great time to introduce myself for the first time in 10 years. My name is @callum.mccaff & we are all Outlander. I was born in 1996 so we priced the magazine at £19.96.
Available worldwide at www.outlandermag.com
PRINT ISN’T DEAD.
🫂 @lilbieber
📸 @sweetcian
🔗 @freenjoy
posts.alt_text:
Naomi Osaka for Outlander Magazine wearing her custom Nike Roland Garros looks designed by Nike & Kevin Germanier!

Roland Garros’ best dressed just raised the bar again. Naomi’s custom Nike looks were designed by Kevin Germanier, the couture designer turning upcycled materials into something iconic. Clay court season needed a moment like this, so we took her to an authentic orange clay court in paris to match the energy she’ll be surrounded by this week😤🤝

posts.caption:
Travis Scott for Issue 01: “The First Ever” Outlander Magazine In-Print!
GANG, TRAVIS SCOTT IN HERMÈS FOR OUR FIRST EVER MAGAZINE COVER. THIS IS UNHEARD OF, AND BETTER YET? IT WAS FOR OUTLANDER MAGAZINE. I AM HUMBLED.
WOW, what a moment. My only wish in this life is that what I’ve spent the last 10 years building acts as a strong form of inspiration for your own creative careers - I came from no fashion background, I had no connections & I had no idea what I was doing, but, I did it anyway.
If you didn’t know by now, my name is @callum.mccaff , I founded Outlander - and I’m here to remind everyone that PRINT ISN’T DEAD.
Available worldwide at www.outlandermag.com

posts.caption:
Inside the former chapel of a stately Parisian home lies #RogerVivier’s extraordinary archive. The climate-controlled “laboratory” houses more than 50 years of work by one of the world’s most influential shoe designers.
@rogervivier transformed footwear, pioneering the stiletto heel and the curved “Choc” heel. His work for  #ChristianDior gave shoes the same level of artistry and craftsmanship as Haute Couture. Decades later, his influence continues to shape contemporary fashion, with echoes of his designs still visible at houses including #Dior, #Prada, and #SaintLaurent
posts.alt_text: Come take a closer look at #DanielRoseberry’s #Schiaparelli 2026 couture collection “The Abyss” inspired by the mysterious world beneath the ocean’s surface. #HauteCouture

posts.caption:
For decades, #Chanel couture shows have ended with a bridal look, but this time #MatthieuBlazy reimagined the traditional narrative as a story of independence. Matthieu was inspired by a book of fairy tales and #GabrielleChanel’s own story, from hardship to success. A wedding dress wasn’t Chanel’s fairytale because she never got married, so instead he closed with the “revenge dress.”
A tribute to Chanel’s life, which was defined by ambition, freedom, and creativity rather than marriage, while famously evoking the spirit of #PrincessDiana, a symbol of confidence and reclaiming one’s own narrative.
posts.alt_text:

posts.caption:
#Givenchy Spring 1997 was #AlexanderMcqueens first collection after taking over from #JohnGalliano. It actually received mixed reactions, many felt McQueen was too young and too rebellious for a house built on traditional French elegance.
His appointment represented a huge shift in fashion, as younger designers began taking control of historic couture houses. McQueen himself later criticized the collection, but over time it became one of the most important moments in his career.
#Zendaya in @givenchy Spring 1997 and accompanying @philiptreacy headpiece, styled by @luxurylaw

posts.caption:
For his Fall 2026 #Schiaparelli Couture collection, #DanielRoseberry explained that he had deliberately travelled to #Barcelona in the hope that the city would inspire his new collection.
One of the collection’s key influences was the work of #AntoniGaudí and his distinctive architecture. Gaudí’s influence can be seen throughout several looks, particularly those inspired by the façade of #CasaBatlló and the sun mosaic at Park Güell.
Schiaprelli used intricate embroidery and embellishment, including real flowers that were fed sugar water to preserve their colour and flexibility before being carefully embroidered onto the garments. Echoing Gaudí’s iconic mosaic techniques and organic forms, transforming architectural details into wearable art.

posts.caption:
Ahead of #JonathanAnderson’s second couture debut, step inside the salons of 30 Avenue Montaigne and rediscover the work of #ChristianDior in the 1950s, a period that defined an era of fashion and cemented #Paris as the capital of #HauteCouture.
#Dior became renowned for his ability to reinvent the female silhouette almost every season. Throughout the 1950s, he continued to develop the New Look with its tiny, corseted waist, rounded shoulders, and full calf-length skirts crafted from luxurious silks, satins, taffetas, and organza.

posts.caption:
Everyone defaults to the same round diamond solitaire — but the ring that actually feels like *them* might already be written in their stars. Aries: bold solitaire, no subtlety, all statement. Taurus: classic oval on a gold band, the kind worn for 50 years without tiring of it. Gemini: toi et moi, two stones, one ring, because the duality is the whole point. Cancer: vintage band with intricate detail — sentimental first, trendy never. Leo: large halo, the ring should photograph as well as they do. Virgo: minimalist bypass solitaire, flawless over flashy. Libra: radiant cut with a pavé band, even and delicate all the way around. Scorpio: emerald cut with depth and edge, not the obvious choice. Sagittarius: nature-inspired band with leaves and movement, nothing “traditional.” Capricorn: platinum solitaire, built for longevity, never looks dated. Aquarius: marquise bezel set on a curved band, stands apart from every ring in the room. Pisces: moonstone with star-set diamonds, soft and a little whimsical. Twelve signs, twelve completely different kinds of forever.
#JustSpreehIt
Celebrations Simplified

posts.caption:
Everyone chooses fabric for the mirror — but your photographer and videographer are going to spend the entire day capturing how it moves, catches light, and falls.
Organza: stiff enough to hold structure but light enough to catch air on every turn — this is why dupatta shots always look better in organza than silk.
Georgette: the fabric of the slow-motion reel, it clings, flows, and catches even the smallest breeze, which is exactly why it’s a videographer’s favourite for varmala entries.
Tissue silk: a shimmer that shifts with every step, so under mandap lighting it looks alive rather than static.
Kanjeevaram: gorgeous in stills, but stiff — it holds its shape rather than flowing with you, which is why it photographs best in seated portraits, not walking shots.
Chiffon: sheer, soft, drapes like water — if your choreographer has a sangeet dance planned, this is the fabric that won’t fight your movements.
#JustSpreehIt
Celebrations Simplified

posts.caption:
Fashion styling is a job. Not a hobby. Not a passion project. Not something people do on the side because they love clothes.
It is a profession that takes years to build. The eye. The contacts. The knowledge of how the industry actually works across every sector, every brief, every type of client. That does not come from loving fashion. It comes from years of showing up, doing the work and figuring it out mostly alone.
And yet it is still one of the most undervalued roles in the creative industry.
Stylists are asked to work for free. For credit. For exposure. For a rate that does not come close to reflecting the skill, the hours or the experience involved. And somewhere along the way the industry decided that was normal. That stylists should be grateful for the opportunity. That loving what you do means you should not expect to be paid properly for it.
We are past that.
Behind every iconic image, every red carpet moment, every campaign that stopped you mid-scroll, there was a stylist. Building the looks, interpreting the brief, managing the pressure, absorbing the chaos so nobody else had to. Often without a credit. Sometimes without a proper rate.
That changes when stylists know their worth, charge it without apology and stop accepting the jobs that treat their career like a favour.
➡️➡️ If you are a working fashion stylist who is ready to do exactly that, comment STUDIO and we will send you the free trial link to The Studio of Stylist Elixir. Rate guides. Contracts. Portfolio tools. Masterclasses every Monday. Everything you need to build a career that reflects what you are actually worth.
Save this. Send it to a stylist who needs to hear it.
posts.alt_text:
Jonathan Anderson just showed his first couture collection for Dior. If you do not understand why it mattered, this post is for you.

John Galliano was front row. The artisans were the opening act. The silhouettes came from ceramicist Magdalene Odundo.

If you are in Paris: the show space at Musée Rodin opens to the public from 28 January for one week. The new couture collection, archival Dior pieces, and the Odundo ceramics that inspired it. You can see it yourself.

This is not about liking or not liking a collection. This is about understanding what you are looking at.
The Studio of Stylist Elixir is our online membership. Guides. Templates. Live calls. A community of stylists who are learning and building together. Comment ‘studio’ for a free trial.

Save this. Share it with someone who needs it.

#dior #jonathananderson #stylistelixir

posts.caption:
Your portfolio is either opening doors or closing them.
Most stylists fill theirs with everything they’ve ever done - every test shoot, every behind-the-scenes moment, every job crammed in like a scrapbook. They think it shows range. It doesn’t. It shows you don’t know what you’re best at.
Clients aren’t looking at your portfolio to see your journey. They’re looking for one thing: proof you can deliver what they need. If they have to dig through clutter to find it, they won’t. They’ll just move on.
A strong portfolio is curated, not cluttered. Clear credits. Simple layout. Work that shows exactly what you want to be booked for - nothing else.
These are the red flags I see over and over again. Swipe through and be honest with yourself about how many are in yours.
Comment “STUDIO” to try the Studio free for 30 days. Inside you get portfolio templates, guides, a full portfolio workshop, and monthly calls where I review your work directly.

posts.caption:
The difference between the stylists who build sustainable careers and the ones who burn out is not talent.
Picture two stylists with the same ambition having completely different years. The gap is never talent, it is the business side. Pricing, contracts, networking and sustainable growth. Knowing how to price the whole job and lock it into a deal memo protects both your income and your energy. A warm pipeline and real relationships beat cold, panic pitching every time. And a buffer with diversified income is what lets you say no and stay standing when a season goes quiet.
These are teachable systems. Learning them is what turns a talented stylist into a working one.
That is exactly what the Fashion Mentorship is built to do. Six weeks of real strategy and real systems, the business side of styling taught properly, with me guiding you the whole way. It starts on the 10th of January. Comment MENTOR and I’ll send you everything about it 💚

posts.alt_text:
Fashion styling is a job. Not a hobby. Not a passion project. Not something people do on the side because they love clothes.

It is a profession that takes years to build. The eye. The contacts. The knowledge of how the industry actually works across every sector, every brief, every type of client. That does not come from loving fashion. It comes from years of showing up, doing the work and figuring it out mostly alone.

And yet it is still one of the most undervalued roles in the creative industry.

Stylists are asked to work for free. For credit. For exposure. For a rate that does not come close to reflecting the skill, the hours or the experience involved. And somewhere along the way the industry decided that was normal. That stylists should be grateful for the opportunity. That loving what you do means you should not expect to be paid properly for it.
We are past that.

Behind every iconic image, every red carpet moment, every campaign that stopped you mid-scroll, there was a stylist. Building the looks, interpreting the brief, managing the pressure, absorbing the chaos so nobody else had to. Often without a credit. Sometimes without a proper rate.

That changes when stylists know their worth, charge it without apology and stop accepting the jobs that treat their career like a favour.

➡️➡️ If you are a working fashion stylist who is ready to do exactly that, comment STUDIO and we will send you the free trial link to The Studio of Stylist Elixir. Rate guides. Contracts. Portfolio tools. Masterclasses every Monday. Everything you need to build a career that reflects what you are actually worth.

posts.caption:
Your job title says “Fashion Stylist” but the actual job is about 10 roles wrapped into one.
You’re the creative director. The producer. The logistics coordinator. The sample tracker. The problem solver. The client handler. The backup plan when everything falls apart.
You manage difficult clients who change the brief 24 hours before the shoot. You chase payments that should’ve landed weeks ago. You track samples across continents praying nothing gets stuck in customs. You keep the whole production moving when chaos hits.
And you make it all look effortless.
That’s the skill nobody talks about. Making the final result look polished and intentional when behind the scenes you were juggling 15 moving parts and solving disasters in real time.
The problem is you’re doing the work of five different job roles but getting paid for one. Because you don’t know how to price for all of it. You don’t have systems to invoice for planning time or logistics coordination or the mental load of holding everything together.
You know the job is bigger than styling. You just don’t have the frameworks to charge properly for what you actually do.
Inside The Studio, we teach you how to price for the real job. Not just the styling bit. All of it. Rate frameworks that cover every part of the work. Contract templates that protect your time. Systems that help you stop underselling yourself at every turn.
If you’ve been building your career alone and it’s not working anymore, this is where you stop pretending you have it figured out.
➡️➡️ Comment STUDIO to join or follow link in bio 🔗

posts.caption:
How do you price yourself? Be honest.
Most rate problems aren’t confidence problems. They’re information problems. When you don’t know what your market actually pays, you’re left to guess, and guessing under pressure almost always slides low. The quick yes comes back, your stomach drops, because a fast yes usually means you went too cheap. Again.
Here’s the freeing part: the stylists who charge well aren’t bolder than you, and they’re not luckier. They price from information, not hope. They know what their sector pays, where they sit in it, and what their market expects. That’s not a personality trait. It’s research, and anyone can do it.
Your rate moves with three things. Your sector: editorial, commercial, celebrity and e-commerce all pay differently. Your stage: two years in shouldn’t charge like ten. Your market: London isn’t Manchester. Once you can see those three, your rate stops being a guess and becomes a decision, and a decision is something you can say without apologising for it.
So the real question: do you actually know what stylists at your level, in your sector, in your market are charging? Or are you hoping you’re close?
If it’s the second, that’s not a flaw. No one ever handed you the numbers. But you can go and find them, and the day you do, quoting stops being the part you dread.
The full Rate Workshop, pricing by sector, stage and market, lives inside the Studio.
Comment STUDIO and I’ll send you in free for 7 days. 💚
Em x

posts.caption:
It is almost a cliché at this point for fashion people to fall victim to the art world. The crossover is everywhere: runway set designs, museum and gallery retrospectives, conceptual collections, and couture that doubles as sculpture. Even the 2026 Met Gala explicitly bridged the gap by focusing on art and the human body.
For me, the real obsession started this past May when Celine partnered with Sotheby’s for their New York marquee auctions, anchored by the masterworks of Mark Rothko. And since, I’ve gone from appreciating art on a surface level to becoming quietly obsessed. And over the last few months, I’ve curated a list of my favorites. Here are four of them.
Read the latest on Substack: The Citizen’s Poste | posteposteposte.com
#Substack #Rothko #Celine #Sothebys
posts.alt_text:
The fashion-tech world has found itself in the middle of a major controversy. A recent Bloomberg investigation alleged that Phia, the AI shopping app co-founded by Phoebe Gates and Sophia Kianni, has been improperly taking credit for online sales it did not generate. According to the report, the app’s browser extension injected its own affiliate referral codes during checkout—a practice known as “cookie stuffing”—potentially redirecting commissions away from the publishers, creators, and influencers who originally drove the purchase.

Phia has strongly disputed any intentional wrongdoing, stating the issue was the result of a temporary coding bug that was fixed immediately after it was identified.

Regardless of intent, the story has sparked an industry-wide conversation about trust, attribution, and accountability in the rapidly growing world of AI-powered shopping tools in a world where transparency is becoming more essential and less accessible than ever before.

What are your thoughts?

#FashionTech #Phia

Zendaya is currently putting together one of the strongest press tours in recent memory. Working alongside longtime stylist Law Roach, her wardrobe for The Odyssey has embraced true method dressing, leaning into Grecian drapery, sculptural corsetry, and silhouettes that feel worthy of the epic itself.
From custom Jacquemus to archival Givenchy and Schiaparelli couture literally straight off the runway, every look has balanced cinematic storytelling with impeccable craftsmanship.
At this point, it’s hard to think of anyone executing modern method dressing better than Zendaya.
#Zendaya #LawRoach #Schiaparelli #Givenchy #Jacquemus

On the other, a growing sense that something is missing: the fantasy, the excess, the unmistakable “Dior Myth” that once made the house feel untouchable.
The question is where does Dior go from here—deeper into fantasy, or back toward tradition? Drop your thoughts.
#Dior #JonathanAnderson

posts.caption:
I’ve been so busy daydreaming about this collection that I almost forgot to post it. Matthieu Blazy’s FW26 Haute Couture collection for Chanel transformed the Grand Palais into an enchanted storybook world for Gaby and the Beanstalk.
Inspired by a volume of fairy tales from Coco Chanel’s personal library, Blazy reimagined the house’s heritage through whimsical craftsmanship rather than nostalgia. The collection evolved from effortless daywear into ethereal evening looks, featuring sheer mousseline takes on the classic tweed suit, trompe l’œil textures, chain mail, and fluid pastel gowns.
The magic was in the details: buttons transformed from ugly ducklings into swans, heels became golden eggs, and miniature garden scenes were sculpted into the shoes themselves.
One thing about Matthieu Blazy—he knows how to tell a story.
#Chanel #MatthieuBlazy #HauteCouture

posts.caption:
Urban life has built an elaborate vocabulary for consumption. Coffee menus run to iced Americanos and vanilla matcha lattes; skincare aisles speak in retinols, elixirs, and under-eye masks. Yet the banyan outside remains “tree,” the sunbird overhead simply “bird.”
When species collapse into generic words, ecological richness slips from view.
A bird with a name is harder to ignore. A tree with a name is harder to erase. Language creates familiarity; familiarity makes disappearance visible.
Full story at the link in bio.
By Aarushi Agrawal (@aarushi_agrawal01)
#connectwithnature #urbanlife #natureconnectedness #naturevocabulary #folkculture
posts.alt_text:
The interesting thing about sachi zari is that it complicates the idea of permanence. A sari may last for generations. Only practice preserves a craft.

At Shanti Banaras (@shantibanaras), creative director Khushi Shah (@khushishahh9) approaches that practice as an exchange between weaver, material, and design. Her role is less to reinvent its vocabulary than to understand how it might continue: through collaboration with weavers, new expressions of established techniques and, soon, digital records that make each sari’s provenance visible.

It raises a larger question: how does a tradition move forward without losing the knowledge that gives it meaning? By recognising continuity as a creative act, renewed each time knowledge passes from one hand to another and the loom begins again.

#TheCraftSeries #TheEstablished #ShantiBanaras

posts.caption:
In India, antibiotics are often treated like everyday pills: easy to buy, easy to share, easy to take when rest feels inconvenient.
Limited sick leave, rushed consultations, time poverty, and over-the-counter access have made self-medication dangerously normal.
The result is antimicrobial resistance, where medicines stop working against infections they once treated. When antibiotics fail, routine illnesses become harder to cure and modern medicine loses one of its most essential safeguards.
Full story at the link in bio.
By Ria Bhatia (@riabbhatia)
#health #antimicrobialresistance #India #medicalcrisis #pillculture

posts.caption:
Delhi is not a market Lululemon (@lululemon) needs to explain itself to. It has long been understood, just not officially stocked.
The Canadian brand’s first India store will open at DLF Promenade later this year through its franchise partnership with Tata CLiQ (@tatacliqfashion), converting years of airport hauls and global wardrobe fluency into local retail.
The sharper play is distribution: the Delhi store arrives alongside launches on Tata CLiQ Luxury and Tata CLiQ Fashion, making the debut physical in the capital but national online. The store will carry Lululemon’s technical apparel and lifestyle pieces across yoga, Pilates, running, training, tennis, golf, and everyday movement.

posts.caption:
Everyone is chasing new AI job titles. In India, the real shift is happening inside old roles.
Software engineers, educators, marketers, and reviewers are finding that the job title may have survived, but the job description has not. AI is now stitched into daily workflows, changing expectations faster than titles can keep up.
Then there is the invisible workforce: annotators, moderators, and trainers who keep the system running, but rarely get counted in the AI jobs boom.
The story of AI jobs in India is really a story of work being rewritten in real time.
Full story at the link in bio.
By Saumya Rastogi (@saumyaarastogi)
#AIJobs #WorkCulture #IndiaWorkforce #AIImpact #FutureOfJobs

posts.caption:
Long before gelato became a personality type and desserts started arriving in clean-label tubs, cassata ice cream had already perfected the drama.
Inspired by the Italian cassata dessert, it became something entirely its own in India: sponge cake, tutti-frutti, pista, strawberry, and synthetic colour, all stacked into one slice that felt fancier than it had any right to.
For many Indians, cassata ice cream was childhood luxury. Wedding buffets, old-school parlours, family dinners, freezer boxes opened for guests. A dessert that looked like occasion, even before anyone knew how to describe it.
Full story at the link in bio.
By Karishma Kuenzang (@kkuenzang)
#FoodHistory #Cassata #IndianDesserts #Nostalgia #Culture

posts.caption:
Rahul Mishra’s (@rahulmishra_7) couture is easiest to understand through the designer he has always tried to be: a storyteller who sees fashion as an ecosystem of labour, memory, craft, and belief.
That impulse has shaped his work for years, from his commitment to artisan communities to the way he has steadily expanded the language of Indian craft. Those who have watched Mishra move from Indian fashion weeks to winning the International Woolmark Prize and now showing at the Paris Haute Couture Week have also watched a designer grow increasingly confident in his own vocabulary.
That confidence sits at the centre of his latest couture collection, DEVI: The Eternal Muse. Mishra is no longer simply translating Indian history for an international room. He is excavating it. The collection experiments with trompe-l’œil surfaces that suggest sandstone, basalt, and bronze rather than fabric. What holds it together is the way Mishra makes cloth carry the weight of stone. Zardozi, dabka, crystals, stones, and bugle beads build depth and texture, turning embroidery into architecture and couture into moving sculpture.
And yet, DEVI is where Mishra’s defining strength begins to reveal its limits. The handwork is extraordinary, but the idea sometimes becomes too literal, too heavily loaded. The goddess, the muse, the artefact, the monument: the woman is asked to hold so much symbolism that she risks becoming less human than icon.
What the collection reveals is not whether Indian couture can hold its own in Paris. That argument is already dated. Mishra’s craft is never in question. What it needs now is risk: a willingness to let beauty become less obedient, less reverent, and perhaps more uncomfortable.
📸: Chloé Rasquinet (@lookwhatyoumademesee), Arina Prikhodko (@arineeel), Gourab Ganguli (@gourabganguli), Moral moda (@moralmoda_visionnaire), karlaotto (@karlaotto) ,silxstaryst, Mateusz Pielesz (@mateuszpielesz)

posts.caption:
the Napoleon jacket has become one of fashion’s biggest obsessions. inspired by 19th-century military uniforms and defined by its structured silhouette, stand collar and ornate buttons or braiding, the style has been reimagined by everyone from luxury houses to emerging designers.
what once symbolised military authority now feels surprisingly modern, adding drama to even the simplest outfits.
the Napoleon jacket is definitely not going anywhere for a while
image rights belong to their respective owners, please contact us for credit 📸
#TFO #fashion #napoleonjacket #jacket
posts.alt_text:
six years after the launch of Coco Mademoiselle L’Eau Privée, CHANEL is expanding one of its most iconic fragrance families with a brand-new creation: Coco Mademoiselle Crush Absolu.

created by Olivier Polge, the intense ambery fragrance layers grapefruit and lychee over rose, jasmine, patchouli, vanilla and vetiver for a richer, more magnetic take on the original. designed to be less about following the rules and more about making an impression, Crush Absolu is all about confidence, intensity and leaving a trail that lingers long after you’ve left the room. 

#TFO #chanel #chanelbeauty #fragrange #beauty

the Napoleon jacket has become one of fashion’s biggest obsessions. inspired by 19th-century military uniforms and defined by its structured silhouette, stand collar and ornate buttons or braiding, the style has been reimagined by everyone from luxury houses to emerging designers.

what once symbolised military authority now feels surprisingly modern, adding drama to even the simplest outfits.

the Napoleon jacket is definitely not going anywhere for a while

posts.caption:
everyone talks about taking over a city, but YSL Beauty actually know how to make it feel like they belong there.
this time, they took over Ibiza’s Old Town, turning it into a celebration of the island itself. from the local food to the music, it never felt like a copy-and-paste brand event.
it’s becoming YSL Beauty’s signature move. every city gets its own story and this time Ibiza celebrated its laid-back spirit. instead of exporting the same party around the world, YSL Beauty build every experience around the culture that’s already there.
they don’t just bring the party. they curate it around the city.
and that’s exactly what makes every stop feel so different
#TFO #yslbeauty #ibiza #yslblockparty

posts.caption:
Even if you know nothing about Masaba Gupta (@masabagupta), at the very least you know that she’s famous. Some people know her simply as Masaba, founder of the fashion label House of Masaba (@houseofmasaba) that, for the last 17 years, has been making playfully printed contemporary Indian clothing. Others know her as the star of the Netflix show ‘Masaba Masaba’. To the chronically online, she’s the powerful influencer, the person brands go to when they want a cool, articulate personality to represent them. The makeup-obsessed know her via LoveChild by Masaba (@lovechildbymasaba), the beauty brand she started in 2021, which has taken her obsession with makeup and turned it into products built specifically around Indian skin tones.
Yet, as she continues to do it all, there is a change underway. As we walk out of her office, Masaba casually drops a piece of news. “I’m moving away from ready-to-wear entirely. I think spring/summer 2026 is the last one we’ll do. The focus is going to be on occasion and bridalwear.” It’s a pretty big declaration, and a timely one, considering that later this month Masaba will be a part of the FDCI Hyundai India Couture Week in New Delhi. Right now, it feels like Masaba is moving into a new era, one where she’s more of a founder and businesswoman than a niche fashion designer.
At the link in bio, @butoolbegum spends the day in the company of the enterprising designer, business owner, and new mom.

posts.caption:
Fatherhood is a new role for the actor who first got noticed for his performance in ‘Masaan’ (2015). In fact, it seems this off-camera Vicky Kaushal (@vickykaushal09) is perhaps closer to Deepak Chaudhary, the character he played in the film. A sensitive, ambitious young man coming to terms with the world around him. That character, Kaushal says, changed him. But not for the obvious reasons.
“Until then success, to me, was where you reached [in life]. It was never about where you started from,” he explains. “That character, starting from the crematorium ghat in Benaras and going on to work as an engineer in the Railways in Allahabad [now Prayagraj]... He had a far bigger life arc than I’ve covered in my life. Till today,” he says, tapping the arm rest for emphasis. “After that I started having so much empathy for those around me, especially those from far less privileged backgrounds, because I don’t know where they have started from.”
@travellerprasad in conversation with the Bollywood actor at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
Zahra Isham was only 21 when she moved from to Kochi with her eight-month-old toddler. The city has a lot going for it. It’s packed with opportunities, culture, great food, and a buzz that’s hard to ignore. But if you’re new here, it can feel overwhelming too soon. But last year, the algorithm gods intervened. Sometime in December, the women chanced upon an Instagram Reel about the first meet-up of the newly launched Girls for Girls (@girlsforgirls.kochi) community group.
The group, which calls itself “Kerala’s first girls-only community”, now has chapters in Kozhikode, Thiruvananthapuram, Thrissur, and Kottayam. While the format stays the same, the activities are tailored to what members in each city enjoy.
At the link in bio, @melodrama.queen with everything you need to know about the girls only club.

posts.caption:
At some point in the last decade, getting fit became another thing to be anxious about. The 5 am bootcamp, the reformer class, the cold plunge, the protein macros, the HRV score on your Whoop at 7 am telling you today’s readiness is a 43. We built entire lives around the performance of wellness, and produced, in the process, an entire generation of people who are exhausted, tense, and completely out of ideas. Enter somatic movement. It is slow, it is strange, and it asks almost nothing of you physically. It is not even a workout in the literal sense, in fact it might be opposite of one, and yet, according to a growing number of practitioners and the clients quietly finding their way to them, it is the only practice that actually addresses what’s wrong.
At the link in bio, @shereegg30 on why somatic movement is the most necessary thing you should be doing right now.

posts.caption:
You’ll agree: tourism today is ruined by tourists. We get it; you want to make the most of your time overseas. What you don’t need to do, though, is literally leave your mark there, like a cat pissing on a rug to mark its territory.
It’s not just Indian travellers going abroad inspiring outrage and embarrassment; badly behaved tourists hail from around the world. Some knock over ancient art or block a pathway for the perfect selfie, others spend a bender at the Eiffel Tower or strip naked just for kicks. Earlier this year, Japanese authorities in the town of Fujiyoshida near Mt Fuji scrapped their annual cherry blossom festival due to a surge in tourist numbers and accompanying unruly behaviour. Locals have complained about traffic congestion and tourists littering, trespassing, and even defecating in their private gardens (!).
To help you avoid becoming that tourist—the one on their way to become a viral meme—and giving us all a bad rep, we scrolled the deep corners of the internet for the dumbest things people do on vacation—and how you can avoid becoming the problem. @tanviii_05 at the link in bio.
Slide 3: X/crosstownnews

posts.caption:
𝐏𝐚𝐧𝐞𝐥 𝐈𝐧𝐬𝐢𝐠𝐡𝐭𝐬 | 𝐓𝐡𝐞 𝐈𝐧𝐝𝐢𝐚𝐧 𝐂𝐨𝐧𝐬𝐮𝐦𝐞𝐫 𝐚𝐬 𝐒𝐞𝐞𝐧 𝐀𝐜𝐫𝐨𝐬𝐬 𝐋𝐮𝐱𝐮𝐫𝐲 𝐂𝐚𝐭𝐞𝐠𝐨𝐫𝐢𝐞𝐬
𝐖𝐡𝐚𝐭 𝐝𝐞𝐟𝐢𝐧𝐞𝐬 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐈𝐧𝐝𝐢𝐚𝐧 𝐥𝐮𝐱𝐮𝐫𝐲 𝐜𝐨𝐧𝐬𝐮𝐦𝐞𝐫 𝐭𝐨𝐝𝐚𝐲? 𝐓𝐡𝐞 𝐚𝐧𝐬𝐰𝐞𝐫 𝐢𝐬 𝐟𝐚𝐫 𝐦𝐨𝐫𝐞 𝐧𝐮𝐚𝐧𝐜𝐞𝐝 𝐭𝐡𝐚𝐧 𝐝𝐞𝐦𝐨𝐠𝐫𝐚𝐩𝐡𝐢𝐜𝐬 𝐨𝐫 𝐬𝐩𝐞𝐧𝐝𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐩𝐨𝐰𝐞𝐫.
At 𝐈𝐧𝐝𝐢𝐚'𝐬 𝐍𝐞𝐰 𝐒𝐜𝐫𝐢𝐩𝐭 𝐨𝐧 𝐋𝐮𝐱𝐮𝐫𝐲, 𝐂𝐫𝐚𝐟𝐭 & 𝐂𝐮𝐥𝐭𝐮𝐫𝐞, organised by the @indofrenchchamber in collaboration with the @comitecolbert in Paris, an engaging panel explored how India's luxury landscape is being reshaped by identity, craft, culture and evolving consumer aspirations.
The session was moderated by @shefatwork, Editor-in-Chief, The Voice of Fashion and Atlas of Affluence, and featured an esteemed panel comprising:
* @karishmaswali77, Creative Director, Chanakya International and Chanakya School of Craft; Founder and Chair, Chanakya Foundation; Co-Founder and Creative Director, Chorus
* Vikram Goyal, Founder, @vikramgoyalstudio
* @rvaziralli, General Manager, The @esteelaudercompanies India, (@esteelauderin)
The conversation highlighted several defining shifts in India's luxury ecosystem:
✨ 𝐓𝐡𝐞𝐫𝐞 𝐢𝐬 𝐧𝐨 𝐬𝐢𝐧𝐠𝐥𝐞 𝐈𝐧𝐝𝐢𝐚𝐧 𝐥𝐮𝐱𝐮𝐫𝐲 𝐜𝐨𝐧𝐬𝐮𝐦𝐞𝐫.
✨ 𝐂𝐫𝐚𝐟𝐭 𝐢𝐬 𝐛𝐞𝐜𝐨𝐦𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐚 𝐩𝐨𝐰𝐞𝐫𝐟𝐮𝐥 𝐞𝐱𝐩𝐫𝐞𝐬𝐬𝐢𝐨𝐧 𝐨𝐟 𝐢𝐝𝐞𝐧𝐭𝐢𝐭𝐲.
✨ 𝐇𝐞𝐫𝐢𝐭𝐚𝐠𝐞 𝐢𝐬 𝐧𝐨𝐭 𝐧𝐨𝐬𝐭𝐚𝐥𝐠𝐢𝐚—𝐢𝐭'𝐬 𝐚 𝐟𝐨𝐮𝐧𝐝𝐚𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧 𝐟𝐨𝐫 𝐢𝐧𝐧𝐨𝐯𝐚𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧.
✨ 𝐓𝐡𝐞 𝐧𝐞𝐱𝐭 𝐰𝐚𝐯𝐞 𝐨𝐟 𝐥𝐮𝐱𝐮𝐫𝐲 𝐛𝐞𝐥𝐨𝐧𝐠𝐬 𝐭𝐨 𝐆𝐞𝐧 𝐙.
✨ 𝐋𝐨𝐜𝐚𝐥 𝐫𝐞𝐥𝐞𝐯𝐚𝐧𝐜𝐞 𝐝𝐫𝐢𝐯𝐞𝐬 𝐬𝐭𝐫𝐨𝐧𝐠𝐞𝐫 𝐜𝐨𝐧𝐧𝐞𝐜𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧𝐬.
#IFCCI #Luxury #LuxuryIndia #LuxuryConsumer #IndiaLuxury #Craftsmanship #Design #Innovation #IndiaFrance #Paris
@payalskanwarofficial | @sripeepul | @swagata.btr | @anuragty | @shwets.pahuja | @rishimoony13 | @iccrinfrance | @indiainfrance
posts.alt_text:
𝐏𝐚𝐧𝐞𝐥 𝐃𝐢𝐬𝐜𝐮𝐬𝐬𝐢𝐨𝐧: 𝐓𝐡𝐞 𝐓𝐚𝐥𝐞𝐧𝐭 𝐐𝐮𝐞𝐬𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧: 𝐍𝐚𝐯𝐢𝐠𝐚𝐭𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐎𝐩𝐩𝐨𝐫𝐭𝐮𝐧𝐢𝐭𝐲, 𝐄𝐝𝐮𝐜𝐚𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧, 𝐏𝐚𝐫𝐭𝐧𝐞𝐫𝐬𝐡𝐢𝐩, 𝐃𝐢𝐯𝐞𝐫𝐬𝐢𝐭𝐲, 𝐃𝐞𝐯𝐞𝐥𝐨𝐩𝐦𝐞𝐧𝐭 𝐚𝐧𝐝 𝐋𝐞𝐚𝐝𝐞𝐫𝐬𝐡𝐢𝐩

As India's influence in the global luxury landscape continues to grow, developing the next generation of talent will require stronger collaboration between education, industry, and global brands. At the recently held 𝐈𝐅𝐂𝐂𝐈 𝐋𝐮𝐱𝐮𝐫𝐲 𝐂𝐨𝐦𝐦𝐢𝐭𝐭𝐞𝐞 𝐞𝐯𝐞𝐧𝐭 𝐢𝐧 𝐏𝐚𝐫𝐢𝐬, the panel explored the evolving role of talent, representation, and cultural identity in shaping the future of luxury.

Moderated by @payalskanwarofficial, Director General, @indofrenchchamber, the discussion featured:

• Dhaval Gandhi, Co-founder, The School of Luxury Retail (@dhavalgandhi1891)
• Jamie Gill, Founder, The Outsiders Perspective (@mrjamiegill)
• Gianni Nembrini, Fashion Design & Fashion Product Program Leader, Instituto Marangoni Paris (@gianninembrini)

𝐊𝐞𝐲 𝐝𝐢𝐬𝐜𝐮𝐬𝐬𝐢𝐨𝐧 𝐡𝐢𝐠𝐡𝐥𝐢𝐠𝐡𝐭𝐬:

✨ Stronger collaboration between academia and industry is key to nurturing future talent.
✨ Indian talent is recognised globally for its craftsmanship, creativity, and cultural storytelling.
✨ Greater representation of Indian professionals in global luxury brands remains an important opportunity.
✨ As luxury brands expand in India, local talent and market understanding are becoming increasingly valuable.

Watch the full conversation here: https://tinyurl.com/y3c34vck
